content.problemStatement:
This is a sequel to the problem "Move the Coins" with a couple of twists!

Alice and Bob are playing a board game. The board is a tree TboardT_{\textit{board}}TboardвАЛ with NNN nodes (numbered 111 through NNN). The game involves moving some coins around the board. For a node RRR (1вЙ§RвЙ§N1 \le R \le N1вЙ§RвЙ§N), let's define a game g(Tboard,R)g(T_{\textit{board}}, R)g(TboardвАЛ,R) with the following rules:


Initially, a copy of TboardT_{\textit{board}}TboardвАЛ is taken and for each valid vvv, cvc_vcvвАЛ coins are placed in the node vvv.
Bob and Alice alternate turns; Alice plays first.
In each turn, the current player must take a single coin from some node other than RRR (let's denote it by CCC) and move it to another node which lies on the path between RRR and CCC (including RRR, but not including CCC).
If a player cannot move a coin during their turn (i.e. all the coins are in the node RRR), this player loses the game.
For example, for the board in the figure below and R=1R=1R=1:



The current player may take a coin from one of the nodes 222, 444 or 555 (if there were any coins in node 333, taking one of those would also be an option). If they take a coin from node 555, they can move it XXX steps towards the node R=1R=1R=1, where XXX is either 111, 222 or 333 and the coin ends up in the node 444, 222 or 111, respectively:



Let's assume that they pick X=2X=2X=2, so they move the coin to node 222. At the end of this turn, the board is



and it is the other player's turn.

Both players play optimally. Bob knows some elementary Game Theory, so he is able to very quickly predict the winner of any game. Unimpressed, Alice gives him a harder task: she challenges him to find the winner of g(Tboard,R)g(T_{\textit{board}}, R)g(TboardвАЛ,R) for each valid RRR (1вЙ§RвЙ§N1 \le R \le N1вЙ§RвЙ§N).

Can you help Bob figure out the answer? Specifically, if WWW is the set of all valid RRR such that the winner of g(Tboard,R)g(T_{\textit{board}}, R)g(TboardвАЛ,R) is Bob, then you must calculate вИСRвИИW2R\sum_{R \in W} 2^RвИСRвИИWвАЛ2R modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 300,000300,000300,000
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
Each of the next NвИТ1N-1NвИТ1 lines contains two space-separated integers uuu and vvv denoting that nodes uuu and vvv are connected by an edge.
The last line contains NNN space-separated integers c1,c2,вА¶,cNc_1, c_2, \ldots, c_Nc1вАЛ,c2вАЛ,вА¶,cNвАЛ.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer вАХ the sum of 2R2^R2R over all valid RRR such that the winner of g(Tboard,R)g(T_{\textit{board}}, R)g(TboardвАЛ,R) is Bob, modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Although the tree TboardT_{\textit{board}}TboardвАЛ is undirected, arrows have been added along edges for clarity, indicating the directions in which coins must be moved for each RRR. The node RRR itself is always highlighted in red.
Example case 1: The original TboardT_{\textit{board}}TboardвАЛ looks like this:

Let's examine the games for R=1,2,3R = 1,2,3R=1,2,3.

For R=1R = 1R=1, Alice turns out to be the winner. On the first turn, she has two possible moves: move the coin from node 333 to node 222 or to node 111. If she does the latter, then at the end of her turn, all coins are in node RRR, meaning that Bob is unable to move a coin during the next turn and Alice is the winner; since she is playing optimally, she does precisely that :)
For R=2R = 2R=2: Bob gets his revenge this time! Alice has two possible initial moves: move the coin from node 111 to node 222, or move the coin from node 333 to node 222. In either case, at the end of her turn, there is exactly one coin remaining in a node other than RRR. Bob merely has to move this coin to RRR to leave Alice with no possible moves on her turn.
For R=3R = 3R=3, Alice wins again, using very similar reasoning to the R=1R = 1R=1 case (her winning move this time around is to move the coin from node 111 to node 333).
We've figured out the winner of g(Tboard,R)g(T_{\textit{board}}, R)g(TboardвАЛ,R) for all valid RRR, and it turns out that Bob only wins one game, when R=2R=2R=2; thus the answer is 22вАЙ%вАЙ(109+7)=42^2 \,\%\, (10^9+7) = 422%(109+7)=4.
Example case 2: The original TboardT_{\textit{board}}TboardвАЛ looks like this:

The boards for R=1,2,3,4,5,6R = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6R=1,2,3,4,5,6 are, respectively:






It can be proved that Bob wins g(Tboard,1)g(T_{\textit{board}}, 1)g(TboardвАЛ,1), g(Tboard,2)g(T_{\textit{board}}, 2)g(TboardвАЛ,2) and g(Tboard,3)g(T_{\textit{board}}, 3)g(TboardвАЛ,3), while Alice wins g(Tboard,4)g(T_{\textit{board}}, 4)g(TboardвАЛ,4), g(Tboard,5)g(T_{\textit{board}}, 5)g(TboardвАЛ,5) and g(Tboard,6)g(T_{\textit{board}}, 6)g(TboardвАЛ,6). The answer is (21+22+23)вАЙ%вАЙ(109+7)=14(2^1 + 2^2 + 2^3) \,\%\, (10^9+7) = 14(21+22+23)%(109+7)=14.
